coredata.dc:title: Doxorubicin-loaded polymeric micelles decorated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for targeted breast cancer therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Mahani, Mohamad
Bahmanpouri, Maral
Khakbaz, Faeze
Divsar, Faten
coredata.dc:description: A pH-tunable “on-off” module polymeric micelles (PM) composed of two parts, a hydrophilic (polyethylene glycol) shell and a hydrophobic (resorcinol) core, was synthesized. Doxorubicin (DOX) was encapsulated into the micelle core via hydrophobic interaction. Due to the low toxicity, good fluorescence emission, water solubility, and biocompatibility, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) were used as labels to form PMs with robust fluorescence characteristics. NCDs were synthesized (particle size of 2.26 nm and fluorescence quantum yield of 8.2%). The prepared PM with 28 nm in size demonstrated desirable DOX-loading content of 33.64% and encapsulation efficiency of 48.90%. Analyses such as FT-IR, dynamic light scattering, and transmission electron microscopy were used to determine the characteristics of prepared fluorescence drug-loaded micelles (NCD@DOX/PM). The developed nanocarrier revealed a sustained release profile where 94.0% of the encapsulated DOX was released within 24 h in pH 5.0. Moreover, the in vitro cytotoxicity test of NCD@DOX/PM showed about 30% cell viability on MCF-7 cells at the maximum dose of 4.5 μg/mL. Overall, all these appealing attributes make micelles a promising modality for the delivery and controlled release of DOX in tumor cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polymeric micelles
Anticancer drug
Doxorubicin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PEG-DTPA as the second precursor
In vitro DOX release from NCD@DOX/PM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical characterization of NCDs. (A) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of 0.4 mg/mL NCDs (λex = 365 nm), (B) Fluorescence spectra of NCDs at different excitation wavelengths, (C) TEM image, (D) statistically distributed sizes of NCDs obtained by DLS in aqueous media at 25 °C, (E) FT-IR spectra of NCDs.

The synthesis of pH-sensitive polymeric micelles by the spontaneous arrangement of the block copolymers in the water solution. (a) The precursor polymer synthesized by adding resorcinol and 1,4-cyclohexyl divinyl ether in the same molar value to the 1% PPTS in THF (N2, 12 h, 55% yield), (b) PEG-DTPA copolymer synthesized by esterification polymerization, (c) 1% PPTS was again utilized to catalyze the reaction between precursor polymer and monohydroxy terminate groups of PEG-DPTA for the synthesis of the block copolymer micelles (see the experimental section for the detailed description).
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) TEM image of the block copolymer micelle.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of (a) free drug, (b) drug-loaded micelles, and (c) blank PM solutions.
Release profiles of DOX from NCD@DOX/PM micelles in PBS (pH 5.4) at 37 °C for 24 h.

DOX release from the nanocarrier at (a) pH 5.0 and (b) pH 7.4. (points, indicating mean values) fitted to kinetic patterns (lines). The model fitting was on first 67% of each experimental release pattern: (A) zero-order kinetic model, (B) first-order kinetic model, (C) simplified Higuchi model, and (D) Korsmeyer-Peppas model.

Cell viability of MCF7 cells after incubation with NCD, free DOX, blank PM, and NCD@DOX/PM for 24 h at different concentration (1.8, 2.7, 3.6, 4.5 μg/mL). Error bars indicate standard deviation (n = 3).
Fluorescence image of MCF7cells (a) and these cells after 3 h incubation with NCD (b) and NCD@DOX/PM (c). λex = 365 nm (magnification 40×).

Rate constants and correlation coefficients obtained from modeling drug release from NCD@DOX/PM through the following: zero-order kinetic model, first-order kinetic model, simplified Higuchi model, and Korsmeyer-Peppas model at pH 5.0 and 7.4.

head.ce:title: Doxorubicin-loaded polymeric micelles decorated with nitrogen-doped carbon dots for targeted breast cancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Graduate University of Advanced Technology, Kerman, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shahid Bahonar University of Kerman, Kerman, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departments of Chemistry, Payame Noor University (PNU), PO BOX 19395-3697, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departments of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, PO BOX 19395-3697, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A pH-tunable “on-off” module polymeric micelles (PM) composed of two parts, a hydrophilic (polyethylene glycol) shell and a hydrophobic (resorcinol) core, was synthesized. Doxorubicin (DOX) was encapsulated into the micelle core via hydrophobic interaction. Due to the low toxicity, good fluorescence emission, water solubility, and biocompatibility, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) were used as labels to form PMs with robust fluorescence characteristics. NCDs were synthesized (particle size of 2.26 nm and fluorescence quantum yield of 8.2%). The prepared PM with 28 nm in size demonstrated desirable DOX-loading content of 33.64% and encapsulation efficiency of 48.90%. Analyses such as FT-IR, dynamic light scattering, and transmission electron microscopy were used to determine the characteristics of prepared fluorescence drug-loaded micelles (NCD@DOX/PM). The developed nanocarrier revealed a sustained release profile where 94.0% of the encapsulated DOX was released within 24 h in pH 5.0. Moreover, the in vitro cytotoxicity test of NCD@DOX/PM showed about 30% cell viability on MCF-7 cells at the maximum dose of 4.5 μg/mL. Overall, all these appealing attributes make micelles a promising modality for the delivery and controlled release of DOX in tumor cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is among the significant disease leading causes of death globally. The major disadvantages of chemotherapy are the low absorption of drugs into tumor tissues and the increase of resistance against antitumor agents [1]. Curing cancer disease still is a significant challenge, despite large progress in present treatments being made over the past decade. However, lack of specificity and poor bioavailability is drawbacks that cause poor therapeutic effectiveness. Furthermore, some groups of molecules including drugs with high hydrophobicity, bacterial toxins, radioactively isotope, or oligonucleotides cannot be introduced thoroughly into the body due to degradation or wide non-target impacts. The confines can be dominated by drugs specifically vectorized to target using nanosized carriers that make the drug to be water-soluble and biocompatible, increase the tumor targeting, as well enhance the extent of chemotherapy agents in cancer sites [2].

Vectorization tactics have lately expanded with the improvement of a new group of nanovectors produced by chemical engineering (e.g. nanoparticles) [3] or resulting from the microorganisms such as fungi, bacteria, and eukaryotic cells [4]. In general, nanovectors are a type of drug delivery vehicle that transports nanoscale material. Their large surface areas allow for effective drug delivery, and their small size lets them cross biological obstacles and carry their cargo into cells. Nanovectors are mainly appropriate to transportation drugs that have poor solubility or a short half-life and have many biological uses including tumor treatment, stabilization, and safety of molecules such as proteins, peptides, and DNA, analysis of environmental dangers, protein and gene delivery, action as self-regulated devices bio-recognizable systems and stimuli-controlled vectors [5]. Over the years, several nanovectors have been formulated and are being investigated, including liposomes, micelles, dendrimers, nanocrystals, microspheres, gels, cyclodextrins, and others [6–8].

Among these strategies, polymeric micelles (PMs) nanovectors are an emergent group of drug delivery platforms. Their ability to carry a range of payloads-including small molecules, nucleic acids, and proteins-coupled with enhanced stability compared to other colloidal carriers, make PMs an appealing nanocarriers system [9]. PMs are formed by the self-assembly of amphiphilic blocks in water solutions. These nanoparticles have a hydrophobic core and a hydrophilic shell. This architecture improves the solubility of hydrophobic drugs by facilitating the loading of hydrophobic drugs into the core. The most commonly used hydrophilic blocks are PEG with a molecular weight of 2–15 kDa, while the hydrophobic blocks typically are polyesters, polyethers, or polyamino acids, such as poly(l-aspartic acid), PLA, poly(ε-caprolactone) (PCL), and poly(propylene oxide) (PPO) [10]. In the last few years, a great deal of attention has been paid to biological and medical sciences, mainly as nanocarriers, due to their numerous physiological and biochemical advantages more than any other. Some of these properties include easy engineering, simple preparing, enhanced hydrophobic drugs Solubility, better biocompatibility, biodistribution, pharmacokinetics, and further chances for additional designing [11]. Furthermore, PMs can be adapted by some targeting ligands that increase their attachment to the specific receptors on the cell surface, thus reducing the side effects of drugs. The drug release from the PMs has to be accomplished in cancer cells or tissues to show efficiently and specifically anticancer effects. However, a PM with a high degree of stability displays gentle and partial drug delivery and decreases the effectiveness of the loaded drugs. Thus, different PMs that respond to environmental stimuli have been established for the “smartly” release of loaded drugs as well as the drug efficiency [12,13]. Stimuli-responsive materials as intelligent drug delivery systems take advantage of the differences between regular and cancerous tissue environments, such as low extracellular pH, different redox ability, and high expression of enzymes and proteins. Furthermore, external stimuli including heat, photos, magnetic fields, and ultrasound can be utilized in various cancer therapies to let PMs pass the biotic obstacles, and release the loaded drugs after reaching the target sites.

The sensitive PMs can be responding to different influences of extracellular and intracellular, as well as outer agents, are expressed as “smart” carriers for various drugs delivery or biomedical imaging, clinical diagnosis, and therapy applications. Since the extracellular pH value in most solid tumors is weakly acidic, different smart PMs have been lately established for anticancer drug delivery [14,15]. For instance, paclitaxel (PTX) is an effective anticancer agent utilized against several types of cancers such as ovary, lung, and metastatic breast cancer. In a study, PTX was loaded in the PM structure that contains a triblock polymer, poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG)-poly(d,l-lactide) (PLA) for the cancer treatment [16]. Doxorubicin (DOX) is also an effective anticancer drug that is commonly used against breast cancer. However, its clinical use is restricted by dose-dependent toxicity, the emergence of multidrug resistance, and its low specificity against cancer cells [17]. Zhou et al. designed a drug delivery system based on micelles synthesized by N-(2-hydroxypropyl) methacrylamide (HPMA) copolymer. A pH-responsive hydrazone linkage was used to conjugate the hydrophobic drug β-sitosterol and DOX to the hydrophilic HPMA polymer structure. This molecular linkage was steady at neutral pH but under acid hydrolysis conditions lost its stability [12]. Zhang et al. produced a group of stimuli-sensitive amphiphilic block copolymers with diverse ratios of PLA/PAE (mPEG-b-(PLA-co-PAE)). The synthesized block copolymers change by self-assembly into micelle morphology to be utilized as a nanocarrier for the anti-tumor DOX [18].

Herein, a novel amphiphilic pH-responsive PM containing PEG linked by disulfide bonds as hydrophilic and resorcinol block as a hydrophobic precursor in the polymer's backbones was demonstrated. Despite limited examples clarifying the embedded carbon dots by PM [19,20] as it is known, this is the first case in which the NCD is used as a label for the development of the fluorescent PM. The surface of NCD was covered with carboxylic acid moieties, rendering them dispersible in the hydrophilic shell of PM. As a model of antitumor drugs, DOX was loaded inside of PM central to improve its anticancer activity against MCF7 breast cancer cells. The prepared nanocarriers were used for controlled release and cell imaging. The controlled drug delivery by the stimuli-sensitive of PMs inside the tumor microenvironment can not only decrease the mass of chemotherapy agents in the normal tissues and accordingly their side effects but also especially concentrate drugs at the cancer site. In addition, different kinetics models were studied to determine the best-fitted model and mechanism of DOX release from the micelles.

All used chemicals were of analytical or HPLC grade and used as received. Citric acid (100% purity), urea, polyethylene glycols (PEG, Mw = 2000 Da), resorcinol, peridinium toluene sulfonate (PPTS), triethylamine, N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), Nitric acid (65%–68%), Sulfuric acid (98%), Hydrochloric acid (37%), and NaOH were obtained from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany), tetrahydrofuran (THF), 1,4-cyclohexane dimethanol divinyl ether, 3,3′-dithiodipropionic acid (DTPA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX. HCl), dialysis bag, and quinine hemosulfate (95% purity) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich Co. (Sigma, Germany). Milli-Q water (DI water, 18.25 MΩ/cm) was used all over the experiments.

Spectrofluorimeter (Cary Eclips, Varian, Australia), UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cary 60, Varian, Australia), FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany), transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Zeiss-EM10C–100 KV, Germany), dynamic light scattering device (DLS) (Nano ZS, Malvern, UK) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrometer Bruker AV 300 MHz (Bruker, Germany) were used for characterization studies.

The convenient hydrothermal method was used to synthesize NCDs from citric acid and urea as precursors according to our previous studies [21]. In a typical synthesis procedure, the mixture of 1.5 g of urea in the 0.5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 7 mL of ultrapure water and transferred to the hydrothermal autoclave reactor. Then, an oven was used to heat the stainless-steel autoclave to 160 °C for 4 h. Afterward, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature and the obtained light brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large and agglomerated particles followed. After that, a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1 kDa) was used to overnight dialysis the supernatant against distilled water. Finally, the retentate solution was kept in the refrigerator until usage.

The quantum yields of NCDs was determined using a quinine sulfate solution prepared in 0.5 M H2SO4 (QY, 54%) as the standard control. The excitation wavelength was 345 nm.The QY of NCDs was calculated as follows [22]:
(1) QX = QST (MX / MST) (Ƞ2 X / Ƞ2 ST)
here, Q is the amount of QY, M describes the linear relationship between fluorescence emission and the absorbance, and ƞ shows the index of refraction of the medium. The subscript X and ST mention the nanoparticles and the standard solution, individually.
We synthesized copolymer micelle which is composed of a hydrophilic shell and a hydrophobic core by three-step poly-condensation reaction according to the previous report with some modification [23].

Typically, into a Schlenk flask equipped with a magnetic stirring bar and rubber septum, resorcinol (1.43 g, 0.013 mol) was dissolved in anhydrous THF (20 mL) under a nitrogen atmosphere. Then, 2.77 mL of 1,4-cyclohexanedimethanol divinyl ether (0.013 mol) was dissolved to THF, and an aliquot was added dropwise with stirring to the flask. The process of polymerization was started through the addition of the THF solution of 1% PPTS (0.033 g, the same molar mass concerning the chemical compound) followed by stirring in the darkness all night. Finally, trimethylamine (two drops) was introduced to the flask to quench the activity of the catalyst and to stop the polymerization process. A purification process was applied by precipitation of polymer in excess hexane followed by thoroughly drying by applying high vacuum till constant weight. The produced polymer was light-yellow oil (yield 55%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PEG-DTPA as the second precursor
ce:section.ce:para: For this purpose, a solution containing 0.210 g of DTPA and 0.116 g of NHS were dissolved in 50 mL of DMSO. Then 0.3 g of EDC was added slowly. The mixture was under magnetic stirring for 3 h at ambient temperature. After adding 2 g of PEG to the mixture, the mixture was left for 48 h to react completely. It should be noted that the carboxyl groups have been activated by adding EDC and NHS. The purification of the product was carried out using a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000) against distilled water for three days. The PEG-DTPA powder was collected after lyophilization (yield: 83%).

Then, the reaction of precursor polymer with PEG-DTPA was carried out as follows. 1.4 g of the freshly-prepared primary precursor polymer was added to 10 mL of THF followed by mixing with the PEG-DTPA solution (1.0 g s precursor polymer in 10 mL THF). By adding 1% PPTS concerning the total polymer in amount of matter, the reaction was started. The solution was permitted to stir in the darkness for 48 h. Finally, with the addition of two drops of trimethylamine, the polymerization reaction was stopped. Direct precipitation of the reaction product with hexane was followed by vacuum filtration. Then the mixture was transferred to a membrane bag (MWCO 4000) for the dialysis against distilled water for 3 days to eliminate the untreated molecules and lower molecular weight by-products. Finally, the products were gathered by lyophilization (yield: 76%). The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy was used to confirm the purified product.

Hydrophilic drugs released from hydrophilic polymer usually exhibit a large initial burst release and a short release period due to their miscibility with the aqueous phase [24]. To circumvent this problem, hydrophobic polymers can be used as an alternative drug carrier. Hydrophobic doxorubicin base (DOX) was encapsulated into these polymeric micelles with high payload and high encapsulation efficiency. The neutralization of the hydrochloride salt of DOX (DOX-HCl) with the base triethylamine causes to change from hydrophilic to hydrophobic form. Typically, an amount of 0.54 mmol triethylamine was mixed with 10 mg (0.017 mmol) of DOX. HCl followed by overnight incubation. Methylene chloride was used to extract the non-aqueous form of the DOX [25]. A concentration of DOX in chloroform (2 mg/mL) was prepared and preserved in the refrigerator for later use.

DOX structure is shown in Fig. 1 . To eliminate the side effects of the drug, the drug was loaded into the hydrophobic core of the micelle. Because drug loading in the nanocarrier does not change the structure of the drug, the drug loading mechanism in the micellar nanocarrier is based on physical absorption.

Drug-loaded micelles were prepared by the technique reported in the previous papers with some variations [26–28]. First, in a small round-bottom flask, 1 mL of newly prepared copolymer solution (0.15 g/mL in chloroform) was mixed with 50 μl of DOX (2 mg/mL in chloroform). The suspension was stirred under sonication for 15 min and then chloroform was evaporated using a rotary evaporator. After heating in the water bath at 70 °C for 2 min, the resultant thin layer was rehydrated with 2 mL Milli-Q water. Finally, the solution containing DOX-loaded PM was sonicated for ten more minutes to gain a completely optically transparent solution. The centrifugal filter with 100 kDa MWCO was used to extract non-loaded DOX from the solution. The blank micelles were also prepared similarly.

To determine the DOX content in the micelles, the drug-loaded micelles were lyophilized and then dissolved in DMF. Afterward, the absorbance of the DOX was measured at 486 nm by the UV–Vis spectrometer. After calibrating with a standard curve, the amount of unloaded DOX can be obtained. The capacity of micelles for loading of the drug can be evaluated by loading content and encapsulation efficiency abbreviated to DLC (wt%) and DEE (wt%), respectively that was calculated as described in the literature [29]:
(2) DLC= (amount of the released DOX from PM/ amount of DOX/PM) × 100%
(3) DEE= (amount of the released DOX from PM/total amount of the DOX) × 100%

For surface decoration of DOX/PM spheres with NCDs, 50 μL of NCDs solution was added to the 2 mL of DOX/PM suspension under gently stirring for 30 min at room temperature. After stirring for more than 4 h in the dark, the solution was purified by dialysis against water for 20 h and the final pH was 7. Afterward, the fluorescence emission of the solution was recorded in the excitation of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro DOX release from NCD@DOX/PM
ce:section.ce:para:
The profile of in vitro drug delivery was calculated using the dialysis system [30]. First, 2 mg of lyophilized NCD@DOX/PM was dispersed in a 2 mL buffer solution of phosphate (PBS) with two values of pH (pH 7.4 and 5.0; ionic strength 0.01 mol/L). The solution was moved into a dialysis tube (MWCO 1000). Then, the tube was closed with sealing clamps and deep into the buffer (PBS, 25 mL) at 37 °C through constantly stirring at 100 rpm. At certain periods, 1 mL of release medium was collected and substituted with 1 mL new equivalent PBS. The release concentration of free DOX was calculated using UV–Vis spectrophotometry. The released DOX was determined from the calibration curve of DOX and calculated based on the following formula (Eq. (4)):
(4)
C u m u l a t i v e
d r u g
r e l e a s e
( % )
=
C t
C
t o t a l
× 100
where Ct is the drug amount in the release medium at time t determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, Ctotal is the amount of loaded drug into the nanocarrier.

Different kinetic patterns including zero- and first-order kinetic models and simplified Higuchi model, as well as Korsmeyer-Peppas model) were used to evaluate the release mechanism and to study in vitro drug release patterns. The zero-order model is associated with drug dissolution that is independent of drug concentration (Eq. (5)) [31]:
Eq. (5)
Q
t = Q 0 + k 0 t
where Q
t and Q 0 are the amounts of drug released in solution in time t and the initial time, and k 0 is the constant of zero-order rate. Equation (6) defines the first-order model that refers to concentration-dependent drug release [31]:
Eq. (6) logC = logC0−kt/2.303
where C 0 is referred to the initial amount of the drug, C mentions the amount of drug in time t and k is the rate constant of the first-order model. The following equation is a simple form of the Higuchi model that defines the drug delivery in the pharmaceutical system as well as the release rate of the drug from the polymeric matrix (Eq. (7)) [32]:
Eq. (7)
M t
/
M ∞
= k
t

here (Mt/M ∞·) is the cumulative value of the drug release in time t, and k is the kinetic constant based on the defining formulation. The Korsmeyer-Peppas model expresses drug delivery from the carrier systems over and done with the following equation (Eq. (8)) [32]:
Eq. (8) Mt/M∞ = k′ tn

in this pattern, (Mt/M ∞·) shows also the cumulative quantity of released drug in time t, k′ is the kinetic constant of this model, and n is the exponent that refers to a specific diffusion mechanism.

However, the best fit mathematical model applied to the release profiles is typically the first 60% of drug release data [29]. In any model, a linear regression was fitted to the collected data using Microsoft Excel. Dependent on the obtained values for intercept and slope of the graph, the rate constant and correlation values were achieved. The model of zero-order rate expresses drug level versus time. The profile of first-order kinetic was considered by plotting the logarithm of the cumulative remaining drug on time. In the Higuchi pattern, the cumulative percentage of drug release versus the square root of time was analyzed, whereas the Korsmeyer-Peppas model was investigated by plotting the logarithm of cumulative drug release against the logarithm of time.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of NCD, DOX, blank PMs, and NCD@DOX/PM against MCF7 cells was evaluated by the standard methyl thiazol tetrazolium (MTT) assay [33,34]. Briefly, the initial number of 1 × 104 cells was cultured in each well of the 96-well plate. One day after the start of incubation, the culture medium was removed and was substituted with 200 μL of medium containing the diverse amount of NCD, free DOX, blank PMs, and NCD@DOX/PM each sample was repeated three times. Untreated cell lines MCF7 were used as a control. Cells were allowed to settle at 37 °C for a further 72 h of growth, and then the removal of the growth medium was followed by rinsing wells with buffer two times. Afterward, 10 μl of 5 mg/mL MTT solution was added to the cultured supernatant, and incubated for a further 4 h. The reacted MTT-formazan was dissolved by shaking at 150 rpm for 10 min with dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO). The absorbance measurements of the sample at 570 nm were carried out by an ELISA microplate reader. Untreated cells were used as the control, and corresponding cell viability was set as 100%. The cell viability and proliferation studies were performed as follows [35].
Cell viability = absorbance of treatment group/absorbance of control group

To evaluate the cellular imaging potential of NCD@DOX/PM, breast adenocarcinoma cell line MCF7 (1 × 105 cells per well) was cultured in RPMI 1640 medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1% penicillin, and 1% amphotericin B at 37 °C in a humid atmosphere with 5% CO2. The cells were cultured in 35 mm diameter glass dishes one day before treatment with micelles. The treatment of cells at a 70–80% serum-supplemented media with the NCD and NCD@DOX/PM at an individual amount (100 μl/1 mL media) was performed during 3 h at 37 °C. Washing the cells with PBS was repeated twice before imaging to eliminate any free NCD or DOX-loaded micelle existent in the culture medium. Following supernatant aspiration, the cells were resuspended in 0.5 mL of PBS before being subjected to the fluorescence microscopy analysis.
The empirical results (average amount ±standard deviation) and statistical values were indicated as average values and calculated by t-test. Differences were considered significant when p < 0.05.

The NCDs were prepared by citric acid and urea as the starting materials through one-step hydrothermal treatment, which makes the nitrogen integrate into the carbon dot lattice during the process of carbonization. Nitrogen doping not only makes the nanoparticles exhibit better water-solubility without extra variants but also improves the photoluminescence efficiency of carbon dots [36]. Fig. 2 A displays the UV–Vis absorbance (black line) and photoluminescence (red line) spectra of the prepared NCDs in the aqueous solution. The obtained spectra of NCDs were supported by the previous studies [37,38]. The peak maxima at 347 nm related to n-π* electronic transitions of the NCD's functional groups [39,40]. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission of synthesized quantum dots was recorded with different excitation wavelengths. As can be seen in Fig. 2B, by increasing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 375 nm, the fluorescence intensity is increased until 365 nm and then decreased. The maximum amount of the fluorescence intensity was recorded with the 365 nm excitation wavelength (at 390 nm emission wavelength). Notably, the excitation-dependent photoluminescence properties of as-prepared NCDs are considered to be originated from surface defects and size effect, which is reported in the related literature [41]. The size distribution and morphology of the nanoparticles were estimated by HRTEM analysis. Based on Fig. 2C, the synthesized carbon dots were spherical with a uniform diameter of about 3 nm. Fig. 2D shows the particle size distribution of the synthesized NCDs obtained by dynamic light scattering (λ = 633 nm, detection angle = 90°). The average size of the synthesized particles was about 2.26 nm.

To obtain structural understanding and to identify the functional groups on the surface of the prepared NCDs, FT-IR spectroscopy was used (Fig. 2E). The extensive band at 3413 cm−1 was attributed to the stretches of hydroxyl and amino groups.

The stretching vibrations peak at 2996 cm−1 is a characteristic absorption of the C–H groups. Additionally, the stretching vibration bands of CO and C–O functional groups could be seen at 1674 cm−1 and 1024 cm−1 respectively. Thus, the FT-IR data point out the existence of functional groups, including COOH, OH, and NH, on the external of the synthesized quantum dots.

The quantum yield (QY) of the quantum dots have been evaluated relative to an standard fluorescent with known QY (CdSQD [34], SiQD [42] and CQD [27]). To calculate the QY of the NCDs, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs and quinine hemi sulfate (as the standard control, which had a QY of 54%) was recorded at five different concentrations. The concentrations of NCDs and quinine hemi sulfate were indicated indirectly by their absorbances. The resulting linear regression equations are also shown in Figs. S1A and B. According to the mentioned QY calculation equation, the QY of the NCDs was estimated to be about 8.2% which is comparable to our previous studies [43].

The PMs were prepared by a three-step reaction using commercial synthons. The precursor diblock copolymer was synthesized by an acid-catalyzed polycondensation reaction of the resorcinol monomer with a divinyl ether derivative (first step). The two monomers were mixed at equimolar amounts in the presence of pyridinium p-toluenesulfonate (PPTS, 1%) as the catalyst. The product was isolated in a relatively good yield (55%) before characterization by proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR).

Then, PEG-DTPA copolymer was synthesized through a facile esterification reaction of PEG and DTPA in the presence of EDC/NHS (second step). The obtained polymer was characterized by 1H NMR and FT-IR. The carboxyl group in DTPA demonstrated the chemical reaction. In addition, the appearance of the resonance peak at 4.28 ppm (h) indicated the formation of ester bonds.
The PM size was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 4b). The TEM image shows that the prepared PMs are spherical and monodisperse with an average diameter of about 28 nm.

UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to confirm the interactions between DOX and PM. The developed nanocarrier combined both the treatment and imaging capabilities made a promising modality for drug delivery systems. In PEG coatings, the hydrogen bond was formed between the amino (NH2) or the carbonyl (CO) groups on the surface of NCD and the ether oxygen group of PEG chains (Fig. 5 ).

Spectrophotometry was used to study the DOX and PM interactions. The absorbance spectrum of the DOX/PM was compared with that of pure PM and pure DOX. Fig. 6 shows the characteristic absorbance band of DOX at 486 nm. In the case of the DOX/PM, spectral changes in the DOX peak maxima were observed. Addition of DOX to PM produces a red-shift of about 9 nm making the prepared complex emerge at 495 nm. The π-π stacking interactions between DOX and polymer origin a significant red-shift of DOX absorption peaks [45,46]. Furthermore, the DOX absorbance at 486 nm was decreased by ∼55% which confirm the interaction between PM and DOX.

The best ratio of DOX: PM (1:1.5) was determined by calculating DLE and DLC as well as observing no participation in the solution. These results showed that the best dilution of DOX was 0.05 mg/mL. DLE and DLC of the PM for 0.05 mg/mL DOX were determined as 48.90 ± 0.09 and 33.64 ± 0.17, respectively.

The release behavior of encapsulated drug from the prepared nanocarrier was investigated in neutral PBS (pH = 7.4) to simulate normal physiological state and acidic condition (pH = 5.0) similar to the tumor microenvironment. In neutral PBS, the release of the drug from the sample was gradual as the drug cumulative release was just about 25.4% during 12 h probably due to their slightly leakage, implying that the DOX-loaded micelles would be stable during blood circulation. However, in PBS at pH 5.0, the rate of DOX release turned out to be much quicker and the cumulative release of the drug reached as great as about 94.0% within 12 h. As shown in Fig. 7 , the cumulative release of DOX from the nanocarrier in pH 5.0 PBS at 12 h was 3.62 times higher than that in pH 7.4 PBS (p < 0.05). This fact arises from the hydrolysis of polymer that occurred through the acetal blocks next to the hydrophilic PEG moieties, which improves their solubility and accordingly their responsivity to acid hydrolysis [38]. Besides, the protonation of DOX in acidic pH remarkably increased the hydrophilicity of DOX. The results demonstrated that these drug-loaded micelles are ultra-sensitive to the acidic condition which is hoped to endow them with a stimulus-trigged DOX release in the cancer cells.

In pH 7.4, it was observed that DOX release was initially consecutive instead of a burst model. The obtained results illustrate that the DOX release from nanocarrier is achieved by a diffusion-controlled release method where the highly crosslinked-polymeric system shows an important role [47].

The release of DOX from the NCD@DOX/PM matrix was observed to be pH-dependent and the release rate was higher in the case of pH 5.0 when compared to pH 7.4. The drug release patterns at pH 7.4 obeyed the Higuchi model that described the release of drugs controlled by the Fickian diffusion process. However, the release kinetics at pH 5.0 appeared to display a high correlation with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model (Fig. 8D). The different values of n for the Korsmeyer-Peppas model were used to analyze of drug release mechanism (Table S1). The n value for DOX release from NCD@DOX/PM was 0.912 in pH 5.0 and 0.595 for the same sample in pH 7.4; both n values were between 0.5 < n < 1. These results indicate that the releases are in agreement with a non-Fickian diffusion mechanism known as anomalous transport, which refers to the relaxation (erosion) release mechanism.

Cytocompatibility of the obtained fluorescent drug-loaded micelles was carried out by MTT assay as a common method [21]. The MCF-7 cells were treated in triplicates with 4 different concentrations of NCD, free DOX, blank PM, and NCD@DOX/PM for 24 h. The concentration of the NCD@DOX/PM was considered based on the amount of DOX existent in the carrier. As can be seen from Fig. 9 , the cell viability was above 90% and about 75% in the highest concentration of the NCD and blank PM after 24 h respectively. At this time, NCD@DOX/PM containing 4.5 μg/mL of drug showed higher cell toxicity than free DOX. Accordingly, the potency of the cytotoxic effect of NCD@DOX/PM was significantly higher than the free DOX. This result was achieved due to the structural design of NCD@DOX/PM enabled the carriers to load a large amount of DOX. The continued release of the drug produces a constant amount of drugs that are accessible to the cells for a longer time compared to free drugs. Consequently, we accept as true that these steady and strong NCD-decorated DOX-loaded micelles can be utilized as an effective nano-platform for imaging and treatment of cancer in vitro. However, additional in vivo studies are necessary to approve this.

In vitro cell culture studies were performed by fluorescence microscopy using MCF7 breast cancer cells to evaluate the imaging potential of NCD@DOX/PM. Fig. 10 displays the in vitro fluorescence images of the MCF7 cells treated with 4.5 μg/mL of NCD and NCD@DOX/PM for 3 h. From the figure uptake of the NCD can be visualized obviously. A vigorous uptake of the NCD@DOX/PM can also be seen. the pattern of nanoparticles uptake is capable of penetrating DOX to the cell nucleus consistently that shown earlier [48]. Staining the cancer cells by NCD@DOX/PM system indicated a high delivery efficacy of the drug into the cancer cells. It was found that within incubation time, DOX gradually diffused and accumulated into the nucleus of MCF7 cells.

A smart polymeric-based system contains hydrophilic (polyethylene glycol) shell and hydrophobic (resorcinol) core that simultaneously improve drug loading and pH-dependent drug-release was fabricated. The synthesized carrier were loaded with DOX and decorated with the synthesized NCDs. The fluorescent micelles showed a high drug loading content that suggested strong π-π interaction between drug and PM. Additionally, NCD@DOX/PM showed pH-dependent drug release. The stimuli-responsive NCD@DOX/PM improved in vitro antitumor action compared to free DOX due to their greater interactions per cell and smarter drug release with pH. The pH-responsivity, biodegradation, and biocompatibility of the synthesized PMs with high efficiency in delivering drugs could be a potentially confident and effective carrier platform for hydrophobic antitumor drugs.

Mohamad Mahani: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Review and editing, Maral Bahmanpouri: Investigation, Methodology, Writing original draft, Faeze Khakbaz: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Faten Divsar: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
